question: Did the review adhere to pre-defined objectives and eligibility criteria?
answers: nan

question: Were the eligibility crieria appropriate for the review question?
answers: Our population includes patients with surgical incisions with no known history of pathologic wound healing. The outcome of interest is aesthetically acceptable scar healing (e.g., native skin match, minimal erythema, no hypertrophy). For the purpose of focusing this review, outcomes of therapeutic modalities for preexisting pathologic scarring (i.e., keloids) were excluded.

question: Were eligibility criteria unambiguous?
answers: Our population includes patients with surgical incisions with no known history of pathologic wound healing. The outcome of interest is aesthetically acceptable scar healing (e.g., native skin match, minimal erythema, no hypertrophy). For the purpose of focusing this review, outcomes of therapeutic modalities for preexisting pathologic scarring (i.e., keloids) were excluded.

question: Were all restrictions in eligibility crieria based on study characteristics appropriate (e.g. date, sample size, study quality, outcomes measured)? If yes, indicate which study characteristic was an inclusion/exclusion criteria
answers:
For the purpose of focusing this review, outcomes of therapeutic modalities for preexisting pathologic scarring (i.e., keloids) were excluded.

Exclusion criteria
Experimental modalities with preliminary data
Follow up time <3 mo
Articles not in the English language
Case reports, review articles, letters
Articles that address unique scar populations
(e.g., pediatrics, burns, keloids, acne)
Experimental modalities with preliminary data

question: Was the synthesis appropriate given the nature and similarity in the research questions, study designs and outcomes across included studies?
answers:
The heterogeneous nature of outcome measures
in the selected studies precluded a meaningful
numerical meta-analysis. Scar assessment tools,
although an attempt to objectify scar appearance
and morphology, are inherently descriptive.9-14
Thus, a narrative review of the available evidence
was conducted

question: Were biases in primary studies minimal or addressed in the synthesis?
answers: This review valued quality over quantity, with the goal of isolating the highest level of contemporary evidence with the strictest of inclusion and exclusion criteria of any scar care systematic review performed within the past 10 years.

question: Did the interpretation of findings address all of the concerns identified in Domains 1 to 4?
answers:
Study Limitations
The main limitation of this study is a scarcity of
randomized, controlled, clinical trials with regard
to preventative scar therapy. Although there is a
large volume of observational, case series, and
case report data addressing postoperative scar
optimization, the quality of clinical trials available
for review is limited by short follow-up (<1 year),
small cohorts, and heterogeneous patient populations
(e.g., acne cohorts plus burn cohorts with no
distinction between the two). Importantly, all studies
included in this study were published within
the past 10 years, thus emphasizing the need to
stay current in scar care. This review valued quality
over quantity, with the goal of isolating the
highest level of contemporary evidence with the
strictest of inclusion and exclusion criteria of any
scar care systematic review performed within the
past 10 years. Still, more high-quality randomized
controlled trials in scar prophyla

question: Was the relevance of identified studies to the review's research question appropriately considered?
answers:
Selected clinical trials were divided into subgroups based on modality: topical therapy (keratin,
silicone gel/sheets), laser therapeutics (pulsed
dye laser, neodymium:yttrium-aluminum-garnet
laser, 1550-nm nonablative laser, potassium-titanyl-
phosphate laser), or compressive measures
(mechanomodulation)
